request_addon_synthesis.synthesis: Add request_addon (verbatim user text) and request_addon_synthesis (Claude interpretation) key-value pairs to session-runtime.json. Detect user add-on comments during work, confirm if ambiguous, store paired entries. Sessions must reference these as live working data — they extend the original request_description with instructions, updates, and clarifications received mid-session.

Confirms the request_addon structure must be an array of structured objects (not a flat string). Each entry should preserve datetime. Already implemented as designed — array of {index, timestamp, verbatim/synthesis} objects.

Approved: implement all 10 suggested session_data keys (todo_snapshot, session_phase, pr_numbers, git_state, time_markers, elevation_status, default_branch, work_summary, errors_encountered, issue_comments_count). Add helper functions and update CLAUDE.md documentation. These persist live session state for crash/compaction recovery.

The session-runtime.json file naming needs to support multi-session persistence on remote branches. The current single-file approach (session-runtime.json) may conflict when multiple sessions share the same branch or notes/ folder. Investigate the naming convention and ensure it supports independent session persistence. This is a concrete example of drift — a feature was improved (multi-session support) but the cache file naming was not updated to match.

Review and update all publications and documentation that cover the session cache feature. Ensure they reflect: request_addon/synthesis tracking, 10 new session_data keys, multi-session naming convention (session-runtime-<suffix>.json), and all helper functions.
Original session request — analysis stage entry point.
Add fallback handling when engineering cycle was never initialized
Implement automatic GitHub label application when engineering stage changes — labels track current lifecycle phase on the issue.

1) Add validation as a request type keyword. 2) Implement the 10-type consolidated taxonomy now. 3) Run the same industry framework analysis on ENGINEERING_STAGES — align with modern SDLC/Agile/DevOps standards. 4) User will manage ticket #521 directly in GitHub in parallel.

Plan a recovery sync ticket session: Martin will provide a scrolling video of the chat, Claude extracts all significant exchanges that were missed as real-time issue comments, and posts them retroactively to issue #521. This is a documentation iteration to backfill the ticket with the complete session trail.

Major architectural insight — the relationship between add-ons, todos, engineering stages, and ticket lifecycle:

1. ADD-ONS become TODOS: Session add-ons naturally evolve into actionable todo items when they contain work requests.

2. TODOS are STAGE-SCOPED: Each todo belongs to the current engineering stage. The engineering cycle manages which todos are active — a todo is 'solving this stage' until that stage is validated/approved.

3. STAGE STATES for todos: Beyond pending/in_progress/completed, todos need:
   - 'n/a' (not applicable) — when a stage doesn't apply to this particular request
   - 'deferred' — acknowledged but intentionally postponed to a later stage or session

4. TICKET stays OPEN until final stage: The issue is not closed until the engineering cycle reaches its terminal stage (improvement/completed). Todos within it are not 'solved' until they pass through the validation→review→approval flow.

5. FLEXIBLE CYCLE DEPTH: Sometimes the user wants the full evolution cycle (analysis→...→improvement). Sometimes they need it NOW — skip stages, document later. The system must support both: full-cycle rigor AND fast-track delivery with deferred documentation.

6. DOCUMENTATION as recovery: When stages are skipped for speed, the review/documentation stage catches up retroactively — the 'recovery sync ticket session' pattern.

This is the missing link between the engineering state machine and the todo/ticket system: stages GOVERN todos, todos TRACK stage completion, tickets PERSIST until the cycle completes.

Past sessions in the Session Review interface (I1) need requalification against the new engineering taxonomy (10 stages + 11 request types). Session metrics and time compilations also need review/update to align with the new categories.

Every session cache modification (session-runtime-*.json) must be followed by an immediate commit. The cache is a persistence artifact — unsaved changes to it are invisible to recovery and other sessions. Rule: write_runtime_cache() or update_session_data() → git add → git commit. No silent cache writes.

The Session Review interface (I1) should display user/Claude avatars (Vicky/Claude identity markers) in the session viewer. This is a NEW REQUEST, not an add-on to current work — user explicitly said so.

Critical architectural requirement: On session start (wakeup), fetch the session-runtime cache file from the remote branch and merge it with local state. When multiple sessions ran concurrently on the same branch, their caches must be merged (not overwritten). This is the Strategic Remote Check (v54) applied specifically to session cache files. Non-negotiable — must implement.

Add-ons #10+#11 merge: requalify all past sessions with new taxonomy + adapt I1 interface to display request_type and engineering_stage. Recompile metrics/time from updated tickets. Both todos are impacted by the engineering taxonomy evolution.
Add-on #17: In Main Navigator (I2), make Session Review (I1) load in the central iframe panel instead of opening in a new tab. When clicking session viewer link in left nav, it opens in center panel.
Add-on #18: Create NEWS.md entries for: (1) Session Review engineering taxonomy display, (2) Main Interface session viewer integration.
